evidences.title: Why Amazon (AMZN) Stock Is Down Today
evidences.content: Amazon is down 7.4% since the beginning of the year, and at $209.83 per share, it is trading 17.4% below its 52-week high of $254 from November 2025. Investors who bought $1,000 worth of Amazon’s shares 5 years ago would now be looking at an investment worth $1,263. While Wall Street chases Nvidia at all-time highs, an under-the-radar semiconductor supplier is dominating a critical AI component these giants can’t build without. Click here to access our full research report, it’s free. While Amazon's revenue beat expectations and its Amazon Web Services (AWS) segment showed strength, this was overshadowed by an operating profit miss. Adding to the negative sentiment, the company's operating profit guidance for the upcoming quarter also fell short of analysts' forecasts. evidences.title: Amazon stock split: what's next for AMZN shares? | Capital.com
evidences.content: ### How much was Amazon stock after the split? On the first trading day following the 2022 split, Amazon shares opened in the low-$100 range, reflecting the 20-for-1 adjustment from pre-split prices that had been in the low-$2,000 range. As of 15 December 2025, Amazon trades around $226 per share. This level reflects subsequent market movements rather than the mechanics of the split itself. ### Why did Amazon split its stock? Company announcements at the time stated that the split aimed to make Amazon shares more accessible, particularly for employees and smaller investors. A lower nominal share price can support stock-based compensation and broaden participation without altering Amazon’s market value or the proportional ownership of existing shareholders. [...] ### Will Amazon split again? As of 15 December 2025, Amazon hasn’t announced any further stock split. There are no regulatory filings or official disclosures indicating an upcoming action. Any future split would require a new board decision and a public announcement, so any expectation of another split remains speculative. ### What was the most recent Amazon stock split date? Amazon’s most recent split was the 20-for-1 a...

evidences.title: If You Bought 1 Share of Amazon at Its IPO, Here's How Many ...
evidences.content: ## Amazon's stock-split history Since its initial public offering (IPO) in 1997, Amazon has split its stock four times, with the most recent one being a 20-for-1 split in 2022. So if you purchased one share of Amazon from its IPO and held, you would own 240 shares in 2025. A stock split does not change your proportionate ownership of the company but rather increases the number of a company's outstanding shares while maintaining its market capitalization. So, to demonstrate Amazon's success, consider that if you held on to one share of Amazon at its IPO, your investment would be worth over $48,000, delivering over a 200,000% return. evidences.title: Amazon Stock Data 2025 - Kaggle
evidences.content: ## About Dataset ## Dataset Description The Amazon Stock Data 2025 dataset provides comprehensive historical stock market data for Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), covering the period from May 1997 to February 2025. The dataset includes daily trading information such as opening price, closing price, highest and lowest prices, adjusted closing price, and trading volume. Amazon is one of the world’s largest technology and e-commerce companies. As of February 2025, Amazon holds a market capitalization of $2.362 trillion USD, making it one of the most valuable companies globally. The dataset reflects Amazon’s growth trajectory from its early public trading years to its position as a dominant multinational corporation. ByMatt Frankel, CFP – Nov 4, 2025 at 6:11AM EST ## Key Points [...] ## Amazon's stock split history Over its publicly traded history, Amazon has split its stock a total of four times. Three of these were during its early years while the dot-com boom was still going on, and one was more recent. But here are the details of all four: Amazon completed a 2-for-1 split in June 1998. The company split the stock again at a 3-for-1 ratio in January 1999. The final dot-com era stock split was completed in September 1999 and was a 2-for-1 split.
